## 2026-04-09 — Prioritize backup assurance over additional dashboard cosmetics

The system already proves that snapshots are being created, so the next architectural priority is not more UI polish. It is improving correctness, observability, and recovery confidence across both the DO monitor and the Hetzner backup runtime.

Chosen direction:

- treat the monitor as a health aggregation and assurance layer, not just a live API viewer
- normalize upstream Backrest data on the server side instead of depending on browser parsing
- distinguish snapshot existence from snapshot freshness, repo health, dump freshness, and restore-readiness
- add restore drills because backup confidence without restore verification is incomplete
- harden SSH execution and auditability because the restore path is operationally sensitive

Rationale:

The false "no backups" incident demonstrated that the current architecture can fail at the presentation layer even while the underlying backups are healthy. Future investment should reduce false negatives, improve operator trust, and add explicit proof that the backed-up data is restorable.

## 2026-04-09 — Keep project documentation inside the actual git repository

The workspace root [`CURRENT_TASK.md`](CURRENT_TASK.md) and related planning docs were initially created outside the nested git repo at [`backrest-wiz/`](backrest-wiz). That blocked [`Reviewer-Pusher`](reviewer-pusher:1) from staging and pushing everything together.

Chosen direction:

- move long-lived project docs into [`backrest-wiz/`](.)
- treat the nested repo as the source of truth for project documentation
- keep workspace-root task tracking only when it is truly local and non-repo-specific

Rationale:

This repository is operationally centered on [`backrest-wiz/`](.). If documentation should be versioned and pushed with the code, it needs to live inside that repo boundary.

## First 30 minutes

Read these in order:

1. [`backrest-wiz/README.md`](backrest-wiz/README.md)
2. [`backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md`](backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md)
3. [`backrest-wiz/src/server.js`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:1)
4. [`backrest-wiz/public/index.html`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1)
5. [`backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:1)
6. [`backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:1)
7. [`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:1)

## Mental model

Treat the product as three layers:

1. **Production backup runtime** on the Hetzner VPS
2. **Monitor and restore control plane** in this repo
3. **Shared repository storage** in DigitalOcean Spaces

If you remember only one thing, remember this: monitor health and backup health are related but not identical.

## Common tasks and where to start

| Task | Start here |
|---|---|
| Fix health status logic | [`computeHealth()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:89) |
| Fix dashboard rendering | [`renderDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:920) |
| Adjust snapshot parsing | [`extractArray()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:190) |
| Update normalization layer | [`normalizeSnapshot()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:17) |
| Harden SSH actions | [`ALLOWED_OPERATIONS`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:24) |
| Change restore execution | [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499) |
| Adjust AI restore behavior | [`backrest-wiz/src/ai.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ai.js:1) |

## Validation shortcuts

- run [`node src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:3)
- inspect health endpoint behavior at [`/api/health`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:352)
- confirm degraded-state messaging in [`renderDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:920)
- confirm audit entries through [`/api/audit`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:480)

## Things that are intentional

- single-file frontend
- file-based audit and cache state
- dual auth strategy
- overlapping frontend and backend defensive parsing during transition
- raw SSH execution retained as a legacy path while safer typed operations are introduced

## What this repo is

[`backrest-wiz/`](backrest-wiz/README.md) contains the monitor-side application that runs on the DigitalOcean droplet and gives operators a web UI for backup visibility, restore workflows, and AI-assisted incident handling.

This repo does **not** contain the full production backup runtime. It contains the monitor and control-plane app that talks to:

- a read-only Backrest instance on the DO droplet
- the production Hetzner VPS over SSH
- the same restic repository stored in DigitalOcean Spaces

If you are new, start here, then read [`backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md`](backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md).

## System in one screen

```text
Browser
  -> Cloudflare Tunnel
  -> Restore Wizard web app on DO droplet
      -> local Backrest API on DO droplet (read-only view of repo)
      -> optional Hetzner Backrest API
      -> SSH to Hetzner for approved actions
      -> OpenRouter for AI assistant behavior

Hetzner VPS
  -> creates backups every 15 minutes
  -> writes encrypted restic snapshots to DO Spaces

DO Spaces
  -> shared restic repository read by both sides
```

## Repo scope vs VPS scope

### Exists in this repo

- monitor web server in [`backrest-wiz/src/server.js`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:1)
- Backrest API client and normalization adapters in [`backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:1)
- SSH execution layer in [`backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:1)
- AI router and tool wiring in [`backrest-wiz/src/ai.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ai.js:1)
- browser UI in [`backrest-wiz/public/index.html`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1)
- lightweight test harness in [`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:1)
- DO droplet container definitions in [`backrest-wiz/docker-compose.yml`](backrest-wiz/docker-compose.yml)
- app container build in [`backrest-wiz/Dockerfile`](backrest-wiz/Dockerfile)

### Exists only on the actual VPS / live infrastructure

- production apps and data on the Hetzner server
- Hetzner-side Backrest runtime and schedules
- pre-backup dump generation script and dump directories described in [`backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md`](backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md)
- real SSH keys, OAuth credentials, OpenRouter key, Cloudflare tunnel token, and Spaces credentials
- the shared restic repository in DO Spaces

### Important boundary

The monitor can inspect and orchestrate. The production VPS is what actually owns application state and generates backups. When reviewing changes, keep that distinction in mind so repo documentation does not imply that the backup engine itself lives entirely in this codebase.

## What the app does today

### 1. Backup monitoring

The dashboard surfaces a backend-computed health model from [`backrest-wiz/src/server.js`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:347) rather than relying only on browser-side inference.

Current monitor-side improvements now documented in code:

- backend health model in [`computeHealth()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:89)
- last-known-good health caching in [`loadHealthCache()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:55) and [`saveHealthCache()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:65)
- audit logging in [`auditLog()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:35) and [`/api/audit`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:480)
- normalized Backrest adapters in [`normalizeSnapshot()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:17), [`normalizeOperation()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:44), [`normalizeRepo()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:66), and [`normalizeDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:80)
- degraded-state UX in [`renderDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:920)
- SSH hardening in [`ALLOWED_OPERATIONS`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:24), [`safeShellArg()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:79), and [`execWithTimeout()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:98)
- focused validation tests in [`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:27)

### 2. Snapshot browsing

Operators can list snapshots and browse snapshot files from the UI using the routes in [`/api/snapshots`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:403) and [`/api/snapshots/:id/files`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:412).

### 3. Guided restores

The app supports direct restore actions and AI-proposed restore actions. Execution goes through [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499), where audit entries are written before and after action attempts.

### 4. AI-assisted diagnosis

The AI layer in [`backrest-wiz/src/ai.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ai.js:1) helps operators investigate issues, propose actions, and require explicit approval for anything destructive.

## Request flow

### Health / dashboard flow

1. Browser loads [`backrest-wiz/public/index.html`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:405)
2. Frontend calls [`/auth/me`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:298) to confirm auth
3. Frontend calls [`/api/health`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:352), [`/api/status`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:374), [`/api/operations`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:425), [`/api/containers`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:434), and snapshot endpoints
4. Server aggregates state from Backrest and SSH
5. Frontend renders healthy, stale, empty, or degraded states without pretending missing upstream data means zero backups

### Restore execution flow

1. User selects a direct restore or gets a proposal from AI
2. Frontend posts action details to [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499)
3. Server writes an audit entry with sanitized params using [`sanitizeParamsForAudit()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:557)
4. Server executes either a Backrest restore or SSH-backed command
5. Server writes success or failure audit entry
6. Frontend displays result

### AI flow

1. User submits chat prompt in the AI tab rendered by [`renderAITab()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1217)
2. Frontend posts messages to [`/api/chat`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:495)
3. AI router inspects infrastructure context and tool definitions
4. AI may return proposed actions instead of directly executing them
5. User approval is required before execution

## Runtime flow

### At startup

- env is loaded in [`config()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:15)
- auth strategies are configured in [`GoogleStrategy`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:253) and [`LocalStrategy`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:271)
- clients are created in [`createBackrestClient()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:321) and [`createSSHClient()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:325)
- Hetzner Backrest reachability is probed by [`checkHetznerReachable()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:329)
- cached health is loaded by [`loadHealthCache()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:55)

### During normal operation

- dashboard polling and health polling are triggered by [`init()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1334)
- the UI refreshes status every 60 seconds and health every 90 seconds in [`setInterval()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1351)
- health calculations happen server-side so the browser stays thinner than the earlier design

## Restore flow overview

The product supports five restore scopes that align with the infrastructure documentation in [`backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md`](backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md):

- full server rebuild
- project-level restore
- application / container restore
- file or volume restore
- database restore

In the app itself, the direct UI currently exposes:

- database restore cards in [`renderRestoreTab()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1151)
- file restore modal in [`openRestoreModal()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:731)
- restart-container helper in [`confirmRestore()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:814)

More complex multi-step restores are expected to be operator-guided through the AI path, not encoded as a large, rigid wizard.

## Important files for a new developer

### Server and API

- [`backrest-wiz/src/server.js`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:1): auth, sessions, API routes, health aggregation, cache, audit log, execution entry point
- [`backrest-wiz/src/ai.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ai.js:1): AI tool definitions and conversation loop

### Integrations

- [`backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:1): Backrest ConnectRPC client plus normalization layer
- [`backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:1): SSH connection logic, allowlist, quoting, timeout behavior

### Frontend

- [`backrest-wiz/public/index.html`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1): single-file SPA, dashboard rendering, restore modal, chat UI
- [`backrest-wiz/public/login.html`](backrest-wiz/public/login.html): login screen

### Operations

- [`backrest-wiz/docker-compose.yml`](backrest-wiz/docker-compose.yml): DO droplet services
- [`backrest-wiz/.env.template`](backrest-wiz/.env.template): expected configuration surface
- [`backrest-wiz/setup-droplet.sh`](backrest-wiz/setup-droplet.sh): droplet setup helper

### Validation

- [`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:1): normalization and safety sanity checks
- [`backrest-wiz/package.json`](backrest-wiz/package.json:7): runnable scripts

## Where to make changes

### If you are changing monitor health behavior

Start in [`computeHealth()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:89) and the dashboard rendering in [`renderDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:920).

### If you are changing Backrest response handling

Start in [`extractArray()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:190) and the normalization helpers in [`backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:11).

### If you are changing restore execution safety

Start in [`ALLOWED_OPERATIONS`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:24), [`executeOperation()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:180), and [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499).

### If you are changing the UI

Start in [`renderHeader()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:857), [`renderDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:920), [`renderSnapshotsTab()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1102), [`renderRestoreTab()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1151), and [`renderAITab()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1217).

## Intentional idiosyncrasies

These are easy to misread as mistakes, but they are deliberate:

- The frontend is a single plain HTML file with embedded JavaScript in [`backrest-wiz/public/index.html`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:405). That is intentional to keep deployment simple on the droplet and avoid a separate build pipeline.
- The server supports both Google OAuth and local fallback auth in [`backrest-wiz/src/server.js`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:247). The local path exists so the tool is not fully dead if Google SSO is unavailable.
- The monitor keeps a file-based cache and audit trail under `data/` via [`HEALTH_CACHE_PATH`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:48) and [`AUDIT_LOG_PATH`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:25). That is intentionally lightweight and droplet-local rather than introducing a new database.
- The monitor still has some legacy snapshot parsing in the browser, such as [`parseSnapshots()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:446), even after adapter normalization was added server-side. That duplication looks odd, but it is a compatibility cushion while the UI transitions toward cleaner server-owned shapes.
- The DO-side Backrest is described as read-only by role, even though the code can call restore-related methods. The intent is that it does not create production backups and is used as the monitor-side interface to the shared repository.
- Raw SSH execution still exists in [`execute()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:155). That is not ideal in the abstract, but it is preserved as a legacy path while allowlisted operations are introduced for safer common actions.

## How to validate documentation against the running design

### Code-level validation

- run the lightweight test file from [`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:1)
- verify route names and function names in docs still match the code
- verify any newly documented action path is actually routed through [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499) or [`/api/chat`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:495)

### Manual app validation

- sign in and confirm the dashboard renders health instead of only raw status
- simulate or observe a degraded upstream state and confirm cache/degraded messaging appears
- confirm snapshots load and browsing works
- confirm a proposal requires approval before execution
- confirm the audit endpoint returns entries after an action

## Local development and validation

### Run locally

```bash
npm install
npm start
```

Run from [`backrest-wiz/`](backrest-wiz/README.md).

### Run tests

```bash
node src/test.js
```

## Reviewer notes

When reviewing docs or architecture changes, ask three questions:

1. Does the document clearly say whether something lives in repo code, on the DO droplet, or only on the Hetzner VPS?
2. Does the document explain degraded states without implying that upstream unreachability means backups are gone?
3. Does the document preserve the approve-before-execute safety model?
������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������SYSTEM.md�������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������0000664�0000000�0000000�00000035467�15166042467�0012456�0����������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������ustar�00root����������������������������root����������������������������0000000�0000000������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������������# Backup and Restore System

## Purpose of this document

[`backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md`](backrest-wiz/SYSTEM.md) explains the full system end-to-end, with a strict separation between:

- what is implemented in this repo
- what exists only on the live DigitalOcean droplet
- what exists only on the live Hetzner VPS
- what is shared infrastructure state outside both machines

Use [`backrest-wiz/README.md`](backrest-wiz/README.md) for developer onboarding and use this document for system architecture, runtime ownership, and operational flows.

## Executive summary

This system has two distinct halves:

1. **Production backup runtime** on the Hetzner VPS, which creates backups and owns the real application data
2. **Monitor / restore control plane** on the DigitalOcean droplet, which reads backup state, helps operators reason about incidents, and executes approved restore actions

The shared storage layer is a restic repository in DigitalOcean Spaces.

That split matters because the monitor can be degraded while the backups remain healthy. The recent monitor-side work exists largely to make that distinction explicit instead of misleading operators.

## End-to-end architecture

```text
Users
  -> backup.designflow.app
  -> Cloudflare Tunnel
  -> Restore Wizard on DO droplet
       -> DO-side Backrest API (monitor-side repository access)
       -> optional Hetzner Backrest API
       -> SSH to Hetzner for approved execution
       -> OpenRouter for AI assistance

Hetzner VPS
  -> production apps and databases
  -> Backrest backup runtime
  -> pre-backup dump generation
  -> restic snapshots every 15 minutes

DigitalOcean Spaces
  -> encrypted shared restic repository
```

## Ownership map: what lives where

### A. In this repo

These files define the monitor application and its deployment shape:

| Area | Repo files |
|---|---|
| Web server and API | [`backrest-wiz/src/server.js`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:1) |
| AI orchestration | [`backrest-wiz/src/ai.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ai.js:1) |
| Backrest client and adapters | [`backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:1) |
| SSH execution layer | [`backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:1) |
| Browser UI | [`backrest-wiz/public/index.html`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1), [`backrest-wiz/public/login.html`](backrest-wiz/public/login.html) |
| Local validation | [`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:1) |
| DO deployment definition | [`backrest-wiz/docker-compose.yml`](backrest-wiz/docker-compose.yml), [`backrest-wiz/Dockerfile`](backrest-wiz/Dockerfile) |

### B. On the DO droplet only

These are runtime artifacts that are not versioned as live state in this repo:

- deployed containers for wizard, Backrest, and cloudflared
- `.env` with real secrets
- mounted SSH private key used by the wizard
- file-based health cache written by [`HEALTH_CACHE_PATH`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:48)
- file-based audit log written by [`AUDIT_LOG_PATH`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:25)

### C. On the Hetzner VPS only

These are the production-side assets that actually create and own backups:

- production app containers and data
- Coolify data under `/data/coolify/`
- Docker volumes under `/var/lib/docker/volumes/`
- Hetzner-side Backrest runtime under `/opt/backrest/`
- pre-backup dump generation under `/opt/backrest/scripts/pre-backup.sh`
- generated SQL and Redis dump artifacts under `/opt/backrest/db-dumps/`
- backup schedules, retention execution, and any system-level restore targets

### D. Shared external infrastructure

- DigitalOcean Spaces bucket containing the encrypted restic repository
- Cloudflare tunnel and DNS setup for `backup.designflow.app`
- Google OAuth application
- OpenRouter account and model access

## Main architectural principle

The monitor is **not** the source of truth for whether backups exist. The repository and the production backup runtime are the source of truth.

The monitor's job is to present that truth accurately and conservatively. That is why the current design favors:

- backend-computed health over ad hoc browser inference
- last-known-good cache over misleading zero-state fallbacks
- normalized adapters over direct dependence on upstream JSON shape
- explicit degraded UI over silent failure
- audit logging over untraceable restore operations

## Production backup runtime on Hetzner

### What it protects

The production server contains Coolify-managed applications, databases, and volumes. Backups cover configuration, named volumes, and generated database dump artifacts.

### Backup plans

#### `coolify-config`

- path: `/data/coolify/`
- purpose: Coolify configuration, deployment metadata, proxy configuration, internal settings, and related state
- hook: runs `/opt/backrest/scripts/pre-backup.sh` before snapshot creation

#### `docker-volumes`

- path: `/var/lib/docker/volumes/`
- purpose: named Docker volume data for apps and databases

#### `db-dumps`

- path: `/opt/backrest/db-dumps/`
- purpose: point-in-time SQL dumps and Redis dump files generated before backup

### Pre-backup dump generation

The dump-generation script described at `/opt/backrest/scripts/pre-backup.sh` is critical because raw volume snapshots and logical database dumps serve different restore needs.

Operationally, the script is expected to:

- dump Coolify PostgreSQL state
- dump Twenty CRM PostgreSQL state
- trigger or capture Redis persistence artifacts
- prune old dump artifacts

This matters for restore ergonomics because database restores often use the logical dump path instead of raw volume replacement.

### Retention policy

The documented retention model keeps:

- 96 recent snapshots
- 168 hourly snapshots
- 30 daily snapshots
- 12 weekly snapshots

The code in this repo does not enforce retention. Retention is a production backup-runtime concern.

## Monitor and restore control plane on the DO droplet

### Core responsibilities

The monitor-side app:

- authenticates operators
- reads repository and runtime status
- aggregates health on the backend
- renders degraded states accurately
- proposes or executes restore actions after approval
- records an audit trail

### Monitor-side improvements now implemented

#### 1. Backend health model

[`computeHealth()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:89) computes a single health payload with:

- overall system state
- per-plan health buckets
- snapshot freshness
- recent operation failure tracking
- local Backrest reachability
- optional Hetzner Backrest reachability
- SSH reachability
- repository read-test status

This is exposed through [`/api/health`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:352).

#### 2. Health caching

The last good health document is kept by [`saveHealthCache()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:65) and read by [`loadHealthCache()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:55).

If live health computation fails, [`/api/health`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:352) can return cached state with a warning instead of implying zero snapshots or full failure.

#### 3. Audit logging

[`auditLog()`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:35) writes newline-delimited JSON entries for action proposal, execution, and failure events. The recent audit view is exposed at [`/api/audit`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:480).

#### 4. Normalized Backrest adapters

[`backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:1) now isolates response-shape drift through:

- [`normalizeSnapshot()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:17)
- [`normalizeOperation()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:44)
- [`normalizeRepo()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:66)
- [`normalizeDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:80)
- [`extractArray()`](backrest-wiz/src/backrest.js:190)

This reduces the chance that minor upstream JSON changes break the UI.

#### 5. Degraded-state UX

[`renderDashboard()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:920) renders different operator states rather than collapsing everything into a single red or empty state.

That distinction is especially important for cases like:

- snapshots exist but an upstream API is unreachable
- repository status is cached rather than live
- SSH to Hetzner is down while repository reads still work
- plans are stale rather than missing

#### 6. SSH hardening

[`backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:1) includes:

- allowlisted operation templates in [`ALLOWED_OPERATIONS`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:24)
- command argument sanitation in [`safeShellArg()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:79)
- execution timeout handling in [`execWithTimeout()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:98)
- normalized result shape in [`normalizeResult()`](backrest-wiz/src/ssh.js:247)

Raw command execution still exists as a compatibility path, but the architecture is moving toward typed operations for common tasks.

#### 7. Lightweight tests

[`backrest-wiz/src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:1) validates normalization behavior and SSH safety assumptions without requiring a full test framework.

## Request flow

### Authentication flow

1. User lands on the app
2. Session identity is checked via [`/auth/me`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:298)
3. Primary auth path uses Google OAuth in [`GoogleStrategy`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:253)
4. Fallback local auth exists in [`LocalStrategy`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:271)

### Dashboard flow

1. Frontend bootstraps in [`init()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1334)
2. Browser requests health, status, operations, containers, snapshots, and models
3. Server fans out to Backrest and SSH integrations
4. Server computes health centrally
5. Frontend renders health and degraded-state cues

### AI request flow

1. User submits a prompt from the AI tab
2. Frontend sends chat payload to [`/api/chat`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:495)
3. AI layer decides whether to answer directly or propose actions
4. Proposed actions are shown in the UI and wait for explicit approval

### Direct action flow

1. User triggers a restore or restart from the UI
2. Frontend posts to [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499)
3. Server writes audit event
4. Server executes Backrest restore or SSH action
5. Server writes success or failure event
6. UI renders result

## Runtime flow

### On server startup

- env is loaded
- Express and session middleware are installed
- auth strategies are configured
- Backrest and SSH clients are created
- Hetzner Backrest reachability is probed
- health cache is loaded from disk if present

### On each health refresh

- local Backrest config is queried
- optional Hetzner Backrest config is queried
- SSH reachability is tested
- snapshots are fetched and mapped into plan buckets
- operations history is inspected for failures
- an overall state is calculated and optionally cached

### On each restore execution

- params are sanitized for logging
- the action is translated into a Backrest restore or SSH command
- execution result is normalized and returned to the UI
- audit trail is appended regardless of success or failure

## Restore flow by scope

### Full server rebuild

This is mostly an infrastructure runbook, not a single-click repo feature. The operator uses repository snapshots plus production runbooks to rebuild a new host, restore Coolify config, restore volumes, restore databases, and reconcile IP-dependent settings.

### Project / app / container restore

These paths usually combine:

- identifying the affected Coolify app or container
- selecting an appropriate snapshot
- restoring config or volume data
- restarting affected containers

### Database restore

The current UI includes direct database restore affordances in [`renderRestoreTab()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:1151), but actual execution still passes through the generic action endpoint in [`/api/execute`](backrest-wiz/src/server.js:499).

### File restore

The current UI can browse snapshots and restore targeted paths. Snapshot browsing is handled by [`openFileBrowser()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:657) and file-restore submission is handled by [`confirmRestore()`](backrest-wiz/public/index.html:814).

## Intentional idiosyncrasies

These can look like mistakes if you do not know the context:

- The UI is a no-build single HTML file. That is intentional for droplet simplicity and low deployment overhead.
- The browser still contains some defensive response parsing, even though server-side normalization exists. That overlap is deliberate during transition.
- The system uses file-based audit and cache state instead of a database. That is a deliberate tradeoff to keep the monitor lightweight.
- The monitor exposes direct restore and raw SSH-adjacent capabilities, but still keeps an explicit approve-before-execute model.
- Google OAuth is primary, but local auth fallback exists because backup access should not be entirely blocked by third-party SSO outages.

## Validation approach for developers

### Fast validation

- run [`node src/test.js`](backrest-wiz/src/test.js:3)
- verify docs and code agree on route names and file ownership

### Manual validation

- verify login works
- verify the dashboard shows health cards and connectivity indicators
- verify a temporary upstream failure produces degraded or cached messaging rather than fake empty state
- verify snapshots and file browsing still work
- verify action execution writes audit entries

### Reviewer checklist

- Does the doc make a clear distinction between repo code and VPS-only runtime assets?
- Does the doc describe the new monitor-side improvements accurately?
- Does the doc explain why degraded monitor state does not automatically mean backup loss?
- Does the doc point a new developer to concrete files and validation steps?

## Problem

The current backup platform is functional, but it has several architectural gaps across both the remote monitor on [`backrest-wiz`](README.md) and the production backup runtime documented in [`SYSTEM.md`](SYSTEM.md). The recent false "no backups" report exposed a broader issue: the system can produce correct backups while the monitor still presents misleading status. That means observability, verification, and recovery confidence are not yet at the same maturity level as raw backup execution.

The main weaknesses visible from the current design are:

- status and snapshot health are inferred indirectly rather than from a normalized health model
- the monitor depends on live API reachability and loosely-shaped API responses
- the restore wizard has no explicit degraded-mode UX for partial outages
- backup success is not clearly separated from backup freshness, repository health, restore-readiness, and target-server reachability
- restore operations are powerful, but they are not backed by a formal validation or restore-drill workflow
- the VPS-side backup runtime appears operational, but there is no documented end-to-end verification layer beyond snapshot existence

## Solution

Improve the system in two coordinated tracks:

1. **Remote monitor hardening** on [`src/server.js`](src/server.js), [`src/backrest.js`](src/backrest.js), [`src/ssh.js`](src/ssh.js), and [`public/index.html`](public/index.html)
2. **Backup-runtime assurance** on the actual Hetzner/Backrest/Restic side described in [`SYSTEM.md`](SYSTEM.md)

The recommended direction is to move from a "live dashboard" model to a **backup assurance system** with explicit signals for:

- repository reachability
- repo health / prune / check state
- latest successful snapshot by plan
- age of latest successful snapshot by plan
- operation failures by plan
- source data production health (for example SQL dump freshness)
- restore-readiness confidence
- SSH reachability to production
- Backrest API reachability on both local and production sides

## Recommended Improvements

### 1. Add a normalized health model in the monitor

The monitor should stop deriving status from ad hoc frontend logic and instead expose a backend-computed health payload from [`src/server.js`](src/server.js).

Recommended additions:

- `backup_health.overall`
- `backup_health.by_plan.coolify-config`
- `backup_health.by_plan.db-dumps`
- `backup_health.by_plan.docker-volumes`
- freshness timestamps
- last success timestamp
- last failure timestamp
- consecutive failure count
- SSH reachability
- local Backrest reachability
- Hetzner Backrest reachability
- repository read-test status

This removes fragile frontend inference from [`public/index.html`](public/index.html) and gives the dashboard a single trustworthy source of truth.

### 2. Add explicit degraded-state UX

The UI in [`public/index.html`](public/index.html) should distinguish between:

- no snapshots exist
- snapshots exist but cannot be fetched
- repo is reachable but stale
- repo is healthy but production server is unreachable
- backups are healthy but restore SSH path is degraded

This directly addresses the class of incident where the system is working but the operator sees a false red state.

### 3. Cache the latest good health snapshot on the monitor

The Wizard currently appears highly dependent on live upstream availability. Add a cached last-known-good health document on the DO droplet so the monitor can show:

- latest live status
- last successful observation time
- latest known snapshots count
- warning that current upstream polling failed

This avoids turning a temporary API/network issue into a misleading zero-state dashboard.

### 4. Add typed adapters around Backrest responses

[`src/backrest.js`](src/backrest.js) currently passes upstream JSON through almost directly. The next improvement is to normalize responses server-side rather than relying on parsing heuristics in the browser.

Recommended approach:

- normalize snapshot objects into one internal schema
- normalize operation objects into one internal schema
- normalize config/repos into one internal schema
- isolate upstream response-shape drift in one file only

This would reduce future breakage when Backrest changes field names or envelope shapes.

### 5. Add source-side freshness checks for dump generation

The backup system on the VPS should not only verify snapshots exist. It should verify that the inputs to those snapshots are fresh.

Examples:

- age of latest [`coolify-db-latest.sql`](SYSTEM.md:108)
- age of latest [`user-postgres-latest.sql`](SYSTEM.md:109)
- age of latest Redis dump files in [`/opt/backrest/db-dumps`](SYSTEM.md:106)
- successful completion marker from [`pre-backup.sh`](SYSTEM.md:114)

This prevents a failure mode where snapshots are technically succeeding but only contain stale or incomplete dump artifacts.

### 6. Add scheduled restore drills

The current system proves that snapshots exist, but not that restores work. Introduce periodic restore verification for at least one narrow path:

- restore one SQL dump into a disposable container
- verify dump integrity / table count
- restore one representative app volume path into a scratch location
- optionally run `restic check` or subset verification on schedule

This is the single highest-leverage improvement on the VPS side because it upgrades confidence from "backup exists" to "restore actually works".

### 7. Add repo maintenance signals to the dashboard

The monitor should surface repository maintenance state, not just snapshot count.

Recommended metrics:

- last prune completion
- last repository check completion
- latest total repo size
- growth trend over time
- dedupe effectiveness trend
- storage error count

This would help catch repository degradation long before an emergency restore.

### 8. Add stronger SSH execution guardrails

[`src/ssh.js`](src/ssh.js) currently allows direct command execution once approved. This is useful, but still broad.

Recommended improvements:

- typed allowlisted operations for common actions
- command templates instead of raw shell when possible
- per-action audit logging
- explicit timeout handling
- stderr/code normalization
- safe quoting helpers

This reduces operator error and improves forensic visibility.

### 9. Add monitor-side audit history

The wizard should keep an audit trail for:

- who viewed backup health
- who triggered restore proposals
- who approved restores
- what snapshot was chosen
- what target path/container/database was affected
- execution result and verification outcome

That history should be separate from chat transcript convenience state.

### 10. Add deployment and secret hardening

Based on the current documentation in [`README.md`](README.md), both environments rely heavily on `.env` files and mounted SSH keys.

Recommended security improvements:

- move long-lived secrets to a proper secret manager where possible
- rotate the DO Spaces credentials and SSH key on a schedule
- add a dedicated least-privilege SSH user for restore actions instead of root if feasible
- explicitly prevent destructive actions from running unless a second validation step passes
- ensure the DO monitor's Backrest instance remains read-only at the repository policy level, not just by convention

## Step-by-Step Tasks

### Phase 1 — Monitor correctness and resiliency

1. Add a backend health aggregation layer in [`src/server.js`](src/server.js)
2. Normalize Backrest API responses in [`src/backrest.js`](src/backrest.js)
3. Update the UI in [`public/index.html`](public/index.html) to render explicit degraded states
4. Add last-known-good health caching on the DO droplet
5. Add audit logging for restore proposals and approvals

### Phase 2 — VPS backup assurance

1. Add freshness checks for dump artifacts generated before snapshots
2. Add a scheduled repo health check workflow
3. Add a scheduled restore drill workflow against scratch targets
4. Track last successful drill and expose it in the monitor
5. Add alerts for stale snapshots, stale dumps, repo errors, and restore-drill failures

### Phase 3 — Security and operational hardening

1. Replace raw shell actions with typed actions where possible
2. Introduce stricter SSH execution wrappers in [`src/ssh.js`](src/ssh.js)
3. Reduce secret sprawl from `.env`-only handling
4. Review whether the DO droplet should join Tailscale to remove brittle dependency on public-IP SSH paths
5. Document an operator runbook for degraded monitor states vs actual backup failure states

## Validation Approach

Validation should test both truth and presentation.

### Monitor validation

- simulate healthy local Backrest + healthy production Backrest
- simulate healthy snapshots but failed production SSH
- simulate healthy repo with stale dump files
- simulate repo API timeout with cached last-known-good status available
- simulate empty repo intentionally
- verify the UI distinguishes all cases correctly

### Backup-runtime validation

- verify latest snapshot age per plan is within SLA
- verify latest dump artifact age is within SLA
- run scheduled `restic snapshots` and `restic check` validation jobs
- run at least one restore drill to disposable targets
- verify restore drill results are written back to the monitor

## Rollback Plan

If any of the monitor improvements cause instability:

1. Revert the new health aggregation layer in [`src/server.js`](src/server.js)
2. Fall back to direct pass-through API behavior in [`src/backrest.js`](src/backrest.js)
3. Disable cached-health reads and return to live-only status rendering in [`public/index.html`](public/index.html)

If VPS-side assurance changes introduce risk:

1. Disable restore-drill cron/systemd jobs first
2. Disable repo-check automation next
3. Preserve existing backup schedules and repository configuration unchanged
4. Revert only the assurance layer, not the working backup path

## Priority Summary

Highest-value next improvements:

1. backend-computed health model
2. explicit degraded-state UX
3. dump freshness checks
4. scheduled restore drills
5. audit logging and safer SSH action handling
